meta.note: Pointer-only index of copyrighted modern criticism (spec §3.2). The avatar POINTS ('RGBC's library has Greenblatt for the Purgatory reading') and NEVER quotes. Positions are stated in our words. ~30 entries in production; representative set here.
meta.pointer_phrasing: RGBC's library has {author}'s {work} for the {topic} reading.

entries.author: Stephen Greenblatt
entries.position: The Ghost is a Catholic Purgatorial figure stranded in a Protestant world that has abolished the doctrine that would explain it.

entries.author: Margreta de Grazia
entries.position: The play is about dispossession and land, not modern interiority; 'Hamlet's inwardness' is a Romantic overlay.

entries.author: Janet Adelman
entries.position: The closet scene stages Hamlet's terror of the maternal body; his disgust at Gertrude structures the whole play.

entries.author: Elaine Showalter
entries.position: Ophelia is a cultural blank each era fills; her madness and death are read through changing images of female sexuality.

entries.author: Jacques Lacan
entries.work: Desire and the Interpretation of Desire in Hamlet
entries.position: Ophelia becomes fully an object of desire only once she is lost, at the grave.

entries.author: Harold Jenkins
entries.position: The most careful modern collation of Q1/Q2/F variants and their consequences for reading; authoritative on the 'sullied' crux.

entries.author: Eleanor Prosser
entries.position: Elizabethan doctrine condemned blood revenge; the Ghost's command is theologically suspect and may damn Hamlet.

entries.author: Ernest Jones
entries.position: Hamlet cannot kill Claudius because Claudius enacted Hamlet's own repressed desire toward his mother.
